questions.q: How do you say 'It is raining' in Dutch?
questions.explanation: Weather actions use 'het + verb': het regent, het sneeuwt, het waait, het vriest. 'De zon schijnt' is different — the sun is the subject.

questions.q: 'De zon schijnt' — why is the subject 'de zon' and not 'het'?
options: 'De zon' is the actual subject doing the shining
It is a mistake — it should be 'het schijnt'
questions.explanation: Most weather verbs use 'het' as a dummy subject. But 'de zon schijnt' uses the sun (de zon) as the real subject.
'Bewolkt' = cloudy. From 'wolk' (cloud). Other states: zonnig (sunny), mistig (foggy).

questions.q: What is the Dutch word for 'the checkout' (in a shop)?
questions.explanation: 'De kassa' = the checkout / till. 'Ik betaal aan de kassa.' 'De boodschappen' = the groceries.

questions.q: 'Ik wacht op de trein' — what does 'op' mean here?
questions.explanation: 'Wachten op' = to wait FOR. English speakers often expect 'voor' here, but Dutch uses 'op'.
'druif → druiven' follows the f→v voicing rule before -en. Same pattern: brief→brieven, wolf→wolven.

questions.q: How do you say 'a bunch of bananas'?
questions.explanation: 'Een tros bananen' = a bunch of bananas. 'Tros' is used for things that grow in clusters — also 'een tros druiven'.

questions.q: 'Op welk spoor vertrekt de trein?' — what does 'spoor' mean?
questions.explanation: 'Het spoor' = the railway track / platform. 'De trein vertrekt op spoor 7' = the train departs from platform 7.
